abstraction_and_responsibility.reason: The Game class handles events, rendering, game loop, AI logic, win conditions, and reset state—all violating separation of concerns. For example, handle_events() processes input while draw_game_over() contains its own event loop and blocking wait, and update_ai() directly modifies AI paddle position instead of delegating movement control. Lines 67-104, 133-150, 184-200, 202-215

changeability_and_testability.reason: Hard dependency on time.time() and pygame modules prevents mocking or deterministic testing (e.g., Ball.reset() at lines 47-57). Game state is mutable and scattered (self.game_over, self.start_time, self.game_over_message). Methods like draw_game_over() use blocking loops instead of returning control, making unit testing impossible. Lines 47, 48, 49, 108, 176

comment_usefulness.reason: Most comments are tautological (e.g., Line 43 '边界检查', Line 63 '初始化游戏对象') or generic (Line 26 '游戏方向常量'). The comment on line 63 repeats the code without clarifying design intent. A comment explaining *why* the AI uses random error (line 104 'AI 逻辑：尝试让球拍中心对准球的中心') would be useful but is missing. Lines 26, 43, 63, 104

semantic_readability.reason: Variable names like speed_multiplier and Ball.reset() usage obscure intent. The reset() method in Ball is non-obvious because it reinitializes speed based on time.time() (lines 47-57), making behavior non-deterministic and hard to follow. Also, draw_game_over() has a busy-wait loop that blocks and obscures control flow. Lines 47-57, 176-196
